result:
Begin with the sound of wind threading itself through pine needles, a hush that is almost but not quite silence. I am standing on the lip of a ridge I climbed as a child, though the ridge is now inside me, an internal escarpment I re-ascend whenever the outer world becomes too eloquent. Memory compresses altitude: forty years become forty vertical meters, each meter a ledge where some earlier self pauses, panting, asking the same unanswerable question—will the view be worth the lung-rip?  

I used to carry a cassette Walkman up here, the plastic body sweat-slick, the foam headphones turning into damp sponges against my ears. The tape inside was a hodgepodge dubbed from older brothers: Rush, Kate Bush, a comedian whose punchlines wore out the oxide. I believed the magnetic ribbon was a literal corridor; if I could walk its length I’d emerge on the far side of whoever I was becoming. Instead the batteries died halfway up, and the abrupt cessation of music felt like a small death, a rehearsal for the larger one waiting decades ahead. I kept climbing anyway, the quiet now so complete it rang.  

Today the ridge is digitally quiet—no whirring spindles, no AA cells declining in voltage—yet the same internal soundtrack rewinds: Geddy Lee hitting the high note in “Subdivisions,” the comedian snorting at his own joke. The brain is a thrift store; nothing is ever truly discarded, only moved farther from the front door. I find the exact rock where I once ate an orange so cold its segments snapped like porcelain. Citric acid etched a crack in my molar; I can still taste the lightning jolt whenever I bite down on something too sweet. Pain as timestamp, as geolocation.  

Below, the valley has been rearranged by human hurry: cul-de-sacs named after the trees they replaced, a mall where the drive-in used to be, its parking lot so wide you could park two feelings between cars and still have room for regret. Yet the contour of the mount remains defiantly itself, a green syllable spoken against the paragraph of progress. I realize geology is the original slow literature, every stratum a long sentence without punctuation, read only by the patient or the forced.  

I sit. The air smells of sun-hit resin and something metallic, maybe the blood in my own nose. A raven lands on a nearby snag, clicking its beak like someone trying to decide whether to speak. I remember my father saying that ravens are the only birds who understand the concept of play: they slide down snowbanks on their backs just for the ride. At the time I filed this under Useless Facts, but now it feels like a password to a country I’ve been circling for years. What else have I dismissed because it didn’t fit the homework of being serious?  

The raven tilts its head, a black prism refracting me into component questions. Who are you when no one needs you? Why does the heart keep manufacturing fresh want long after the shelves are stocked? If time is a river, why do we keep trying to dam it with narrative? The bird launches, wings ripping a silent seam in the afternoon, and the vacuum it leaves fills with an answer I almost understand: play is the opposite of useless; it is how the universe tastes itself without swallowing.  

I unzip my jacket pocket out of habit; no Walkman, only a phone that can summon almost every song ever recorded yet somehow makes music feel cheaper, like water from a tap instead of a spring you stumbled upon. I resist the reflex to check headlines. Instead I press RECORD and hold the device toward the wind, capturing five minutes of non-human conversation: needle talk, distant woodpecker Morse, the soft complaint of my own breathing. Later I will listen in headphones and discover the ridge has been speaking in frequencies below language, a slow sentence that began long before the first verb and will continue after the last noun decays.  

Descending, I take a different trail, one maintained by forgetful volunteers—switchbacks too gentle, braiding into each other like thoughts you can’t tell apart. I pass a stump cut decades ago; its rings are still visible, historical vinyl. I count backwards: drought, beetle outbreak, the summer someone sprayed Agent Orange on power lines nearby. The tree has archived it all, a wooden hard drive. I place my palm on the flat surface and feel nothing, which is itself information: the past is not transferrable by touch alone; you have to grow around it, let it stiffen your own grain.  

Halfway down I meet a boy climbing with earbuds, eyes on the screen rather than the path. He startles when he sees me, yanks a bud free as if caught smuggling. I want to warn him about batteries dying, about oranges that bite back, about the moment music stops and you hear your own pulse trying to harmonize with altitude. But he is already past, thumbs typing a text that will reach someone before he does, compressing elevation into emoticons. I step aside, feeling ancient, aromatic as cedar.  

At the trailhead parking lot the world reassembles: engines, plastic, the smell of fried food drifting from a food truck idling in turnout. I consider buying fries, but the line is a congregation staring down at rectangles, no one speaking to the human standing beside them. I return to my car, drive home on a road that braids old logging routes, past barns collapsing like slow sighs. Radio off, I listen to the captured wind. It sounds like someone folding a map the size of a life, forever trying to find the corner that says YOU ARE HERE.  

Night. The house is quiet except for the refrigerator composing a minimalist opera. I sit at the kitchen table, open my laptop, and stare at the blank document already titled RidgeNotes.docx. Cursor blinking, a digital raven. I think of the boy, of the stump, of the orange lightning. Then I begin, not at the beginning but at the sound of ending: the moment the tape ran out and the mountain kept singing. I write until the fan kicks in, until the cursor is a heartbeat, until the words are no longer mine but the ridge climbing through me, placing one foot after another in the dark, trusting that somewhere ahead the view will open, and if it doesn’t, the climb itself will have to be enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
